explicit_text: I was born in Norfolk, Virginia, and lived there until I was about seven or eight years old. My father moved there to make his fortune and worked as a waiter on a steamer running from Norfolk to Washington. Norfolk is nearly surrounded by water and was connected to other places by boat before bridges were built. It was a significant place in my early childhood and family history.
implicit_text: I was born in a coastal city in southeastern Virginia and lived there until I was about seven or eight years old. My father moved there to make his fortune and worked as a waiter on a steamer running from that city to Washington. This city is nearly surrounded by water and was connected to other places by boat before bridges were built. It was a significant place in my early childhood and family history.

explicit_text: Both my parents were from North Carolina, specifically from Warren County, though from different sections. After I was about seven or eight, our family moved back to North Carolina from Norfolk. My mother stayed in her home area and taught school there, while my father had gone to Norfolk first. North Carolina was where much of my extended family lived and where I spent many summers.

explicit_text: Warren County is where my parents came from and where much of my mother's family lived. My grandfather, Mitchell R. Ross, was a minister there and owned land that was settled by relatives after Emancipation. Several family members, including my mother, father, and brother, are buried there. It was also the location of the academy where my parents met and where my mother taught school.

explicit_text: The academy where my parents met was established by a black New Englander who carried on the old New England tradition. It might have been connected to northern or New England Baptists, though it wasn't directly church-related like other local schools. This New England influence shaped the educational environment where my parents first encountered each other.

explicit_text: I became a church member at the age of nine in the good old Baptist tradition. My grandfather on my mother's side was a minister with several churches in Warren County. The academy where my parents met may have had ties to New England Baptists, and my father's brother was prominent in Cherry Street Baptist Church in Philadelphia. The Baptist faith was a strong part of my family's life and heritage.

explicit_text: Both of my parents' parents were ex-slaves, and after Emancipation, my mother's father, who was a minister, managed to secure a large portion of land in Warren County. This land was divided into plots for family members, and I even sent my first earnings to help pay taxes on it. Emancipation was a pivotal event that shaped my family's opportunities and land ownership.

This historic event was a pivotal moment that shaped my family's opportunities and land ownership.

explicit_text: Philadelphia was where some of my father's family settled, including his oldest brother and Aunt Eliza's grown sons. I was going to visit a first cousin there in 1974, but I had an accident at Pennsylvania Station and couldn't make the trip. My father's brother was prominent in a church there, likely Cherry Street Baptist Church, and the city was an important place for part of my family.

explicit_text: In 1974, I had an eerie accident at Pennsylvania Station in Philadelphia where I fell between two cars and cracked my ankle. This injury prevented me from visiting a first cousin who had more information about my father's side of the family. It was a significant moment because by the time I recovered, my cousin had passed away.

explicit_text: During my day, a branch of the Pennsylvania Railroad ran into the Cape Charles area, but you still had to take a boat to reach Norfolk. This area was part of the transportation route connecting Virginia to the mainland and Washington, D.C. It was an important geographic point related to my father's work and travel.

explicit_text: My brother was named Blake Curtis, and I have a hunch his middle name came from the Curtis Publishing Company, which had early magazine distributors. My mother was a great reader and may have sold their magazines, but I never confirmed the exact reason for the name. It was a small but interesting part of our family story.

explicit_text: Warrenton was the county seat where my father rode horseback to court my mother in a nearby area called Flums. They married there after he had settled in Norfolk for a time. Warrenton was also the location of the academy where my parents met and near where my mother's family lived and taught school.
